revision.comment: 妙見宗とは1、1946年12月10日に天台宗より独立した宗派
revision.text:
'''妙見宗'''（みょうけんしゅう）とは、1946年12月10日に[[天台宗]]より独立した宗派。 開祖は野間日照、宗祖は野間秀泉。総本山は[[本瀧寺]]。 [[日蓮宗]]および[[修験道]]の影響が強い。 本尊は[[釈迦|釈迦如来]]（または[[法華曼荼羅]]）、脇本尊として[[妙見菩薩|妙見大菩薩]]（みょうけんだいぼさつ）と[[常富大菩薩]]（つねとみだいぼさつ）。経典は、[[妙法蓮華経]]。[[台密]]と[[木剣]]を組み合わせた法要を行う。
== 関連項目 == * [[本瀧寺]] * [[能勢妙見山]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.hontakiji.com 能勢妙見山 妙見宗総本山 本瀧寺]
{{Buddhism-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みょうけんしゅう}} [[Category:仏教系新宗教]] [[Category:日本の新宗教]] [[Category:日本の宗教団体]]

== 関連項目 == * [[本瀧寺]] * [[能勢妙見山]]

{{Buddhism-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みょうけんしゅう}} [[Category:仏教系新宗教]] [[Category:日本の新宗教]] [[Category:日本の宗教団体]]

'''妙見宗'''（みょうけんしゅう）とは、1946年12月10日に[[天台宗]]より独立した宗派。開祖は野間日照上人、宗祖は野間秀泉上人。総本山は[[本瀧寺]]。本尊は[[釈迦|釈迦如来]]（または[[法華曼荼羅]]）、脇本尊として[[妙見菩薩|妙見大菩薩]]（みょうけんだいぼさつ）と[[常富大菩薩]]（つねとみだいぼさつ）。経典は[[妙法蓮華経]]。総本山[[本瀧寺]]では[[台密]]と[[木剣]]祈祷を組み合わせた法要を行う。[[日蓮宗]]および[[修験道]]の影響が強い。

{{出典の明記|date=2013年10月}} '''妙見宗'''（みょうけんしゅう）とは、1946年12月10日に[[天台宗]]より独立した宗派。開祖は野間日照上人、宗祖は野間秀泉上人。総本山は[[本瀧寺]]。本尊は[[釈迦|釈迦如来]]（または[[法華曼荼羅]]）、脇本尊として[[妙見菩薩|妙見大菩薩]]（みょうけんだいぼさつ）と[[常富大菩薩]]（つねとみだいぼさつ）。経典は[[妙法蓮華経]]。総本山[[本瀧寺]]では[[台密]]と[[木剣]]祈祷を組み合わせた法要を行う。[[日蓮宗]]および[[修験道]]の影響が強い。

{{出典の明記|date=2013年10月}} '''妙見宗'''（みょうけんしゅう）とは、1946年12月10日に[[天台宗]]より独立した宗派。開祖は野間日照上人、宗祖は野間秀泉上人。総本山は[[本瀧寺]]。本尊は蔵王権現、脇本尊として[[妙見菩薩|妙見大菩薩]]（みょうけんだいぼさつ）と[[常富大菩薩]]（つねとみだいぼさつ）。経典は[[妙法蓮華経]]。総本山[[本瀧寺]]では[[台密]]と[[法剣]]祈祷を組み合わせた法要を行う。[[日蓮宗]]の法要形式をなぞった[[修験道]]系である。 妙見宗僧侶の服装は[[日蓮宗]]と同じで[[お題目]]を唱えてはいるが、[[日蓮教学]]がない事、法剣祈祷をする上で撰経を身に付けていないことや[[荒行]]が存在しないこと、また本山である[[本瀧寺]]には[[法華曼荼羅]]が存在しないことなどから、[[日蓮宗]]の影響は形だけである。 一部末寺のみ[[日蓮宗]]の荒行成満者が存在する。
== 関連項目 == * [[本瀧寺]] * [[能勢妙見山]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.hontakiji.com 能勢妙見山 妙見宗総本山 本瀧寺]
{{新宗教}}
{{Buddhism-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みょうけんしゅう}} [[Category:仏教系新宗教]] [[Category:日本の新宗教]] [[Category:日本の宗教団体]]

{{出典の明記|date=2013年10月}} '''妙見宗'''（みょうけんしゅう）とは、1946年12月10日に[[天台宗]]より独立した宗派。開祖は野間日照上人、宗祖は野間秀泉上人。総本山は[[本瀧寺]]。本尊は本瀧寺は[[蔵王権現]]、末寺は[[法華曼荼羅]]、脇本尊として[[妙見菩薩|妙見大菩薩]]（みょうけんだいぼさつ）と[[常富大菩薩]]（つねとみだいぼさつ）。経典は[[妙法蓮華経]]。総本山[[本瀧寺]]では[[台密]]と[[日蓮宗]]の法要形式を組み合わせ[[修験道]]系の法要を行う。 妙見宗僧侶の服装は[[日蓮宗]]と同じで[[お題目]]を唱えてはいるが、[[日蓮教学]]が存在しない事、荒行が無く日蓮宗と同等の法華祈祷の相承が無いこと、また本山である[[本瀧寺]]には[[法華曼荼羅]]が存在しないことなどから、[[日蓮宗]]の影響は形だけである。 一部末寺のみ[[日蓮宗]]の荒行成満者が存在する。

revision.comment: 日蓮宗との違いの明確化
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2013年10月}} '''妙見宗'''（みょうけんしゅう）とは、1946年12月10日に[[天台宗]]より独立した宗派。開祖は野間日照上人、宗祖は野間秀泉上人。総本山は[[本瀧寺]]。 [[本瀧寺]]内にある滝で修行した[[修験者]]を中心に構成されている。本尊は本瀧寺は[[蔵王権現]]、末寺は[[法華曼荼羅]]。脇本尊として[[妙見菩薩|妙見大菩薩]]（みょうけんだいぼさつ）と[[常富大菩薩]]（つねとみだいぼさつ）。 経典は[[妙法蓮華経]]。 総本山[[本瀧寺]]では[[日蓮宗]]の法要形式に[[台密]]と[[修験道]]を組み合わせた法要を行う。 妙見宗僧侶の服装は[[日蓮宗]]と同じで[[お題目]]を唱えてはいるが、 本山である[[本瀧寺]]には[[法華曼荼羅]]が存在しないこと、[[日蓮教学]]が存在しないこと、荒行が無く[[日蓮宗]]と同等の法華祈祷の相承が無いこと などから、[[日蓮宗]]の法要形式を修験道に取り入れた新宗教であるといえる。 一部末寺のみ[[日蓮宗]]の荒行成満者が存在する。
== 関連項目 == * [[本瀧寺]] * [[能勢妙見山]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.hontakiji.com 能勢妙見山 妙見宗総本山 本瀧寺]
{{新宗教}}
{{Buddhism-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みょうけんしゅう}} [[Category:仏教系新宗教]] [[Category:日本の新宗教]] [[Category:日本の宗教団体]]

{{出典の明記|date=2013年10月}} '''妙見宗'''（みょうけんしゅう）とは、1946年12月10日に[[天台宗]]より独立した宗派。開祖は野間日照上人、宗祖は野間秀泉上人。総本山は[[本瀧寺]]。 [[本瀧寺]]内にある滝で修行した[[修験者]]を中心に構成されている。本尊は本瀧寺は[[蔵王権現]]、末寺は[[法華曼荼羅]]。脇本尊として[[妙見菩薩|妙見大菩薩]]（みょうけんだいぼさつ）と[[常富大菩薩]]（つねとみだいぼさつ）。 経典は[[妙法蓮華経]]。 総本山[[本瀧寺]]では[[日蓮宗]]の法要形式に[[台密]]と[[修験道]]を組み合わせた法要を行う。 妙見宗僧侶の服装は[[日蓮宗]]と同じで[[お題目]]を唱えてはいるが、 本山である[[本瀧寺]]には[[法華曼荼羅]]が存在しないこと、[[日蓮教学]]が存在しないこと、荒行が無く[[日蓮宗]]と同等の法華祈祷の相承が無いこと、独自に日蓮宗の木剱相承を簡略化した祈祷を行うことなどから、[[日蓮宗]]の法要形式を修験道に取り入れた新宗教であるといえる。 一部末寺のみ[[日蓮宗]]の荒行成満者が存在する。

revision.comment: 日蓮宗との違いを追加
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2013年10月}} '''妙見宗'''（みょうけんしゅう）とは、1946年12月10日に[[天台宗]]より独立した宗派。開祖は野間日照上人、宗祖は野間秀泉上人。総本山は[[本瀧寺]]。 [[本瀧寺]]内にある滝で修行した[[修験者]]を中心に構成されている。本尊は本瀧寺は[[蔵王権現]]、末寺は[[法華曼荼羅]]。脇本尊として[[妙見菩薩|妙見大菩薩]]（みょうけんだいぼさつ）と[[常富大菩薩]]（つねとみだいぼさつ）。 経典は[[妙法蓮華経]]。 総本山[[本瀧寺]]では[[日蓮宗]]の法要形式に[[台密]]と[[修験道]]を組み合わせた法要を行う。 妙見宗僧侶の服装は[[日蓮宗]]と同じで[[お題目]]を唱えてはいるが、 本山である[[本瀧寺]]には[[法華曼荼羅]]が存在しないこと、[[日蓮教学]]が存在しないこと、[[日蓮]]聖人に関する記念行事が無いこと、荒行が無く[[日蓮宗]]と同等の法華祈祷の相承が無いこと、独自に日蓮宗の木剱相承を簡略化した祈祷を行うことなどから、[[日蓮宗]]の法要形式を修験道に取り入れた新宗教であるといえる。 一部末寺のみ[[日蓮宗]]の荒行成満者が存在する。
== 関連項目 == * [[本瀧寺]] * [[能勢妙見山]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.hontakiji.com 能勢妙見山 妙見宗総本山 本瀧寺]
{{新宗教}}
{{Buddhism-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みょうけんしゅう}} [[Category:仏教系新宗教]] [[Category:日本の新宗教]] [[Category:日本の宗教団体]]

'''妙見宗'''（みょうけんしゅう）とは、1946年12月10日に[[天台宗]]より独立した宗派。開祖は野間日照上人、宗祖は野間秀泉上人。総本山は[[本瀧寺]]。 [[本瀧寺]]内にある滝で修行した[[修験者]]を中心に構成されている。本尊は本瀧寺は[[釈迦牟尼仏]]、末寺は[[法華曼荼羅]]。脇本尊として[[妙見菩薩|妙見大菩薩]]（みょうけんだいぼさつ）と[[常富大菩薩]]（つねとみだいぼさつ）。経典は[[妙法蓮華経]]。 総本山[[本瀧寺]]では[[日蓮宗]]の法要形式に[[台密]]と[[修験道]]を組み合わせた法要を行う。 妙見宗僧侶の服装は[[日蓮宗]]を模し[[お題目]]を唱えてはいるが、 本山である[[本瀧寺]]には[[法華曼荼羅]]が存在しないこと、[[日蓮教学]]が存在しないこと、[[日蓮]]聖人に関する記念行事が無いこと、荒行が無く[[日蓮宗]]と同等の法華祈祷の相承が無いこと、独自に日蓮宗の木剱相承を簡略化した祈祷を行うことなどから、[[日蓮宗]]の法要形式を修験道に取り入れた新宗教であるといえる。 一部末寺のみ[[日蓮宗]]の荒行成満者が存在する。
== 関連項目 == * [[本瀧寺]] * [[能勢妙見山]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.hontakiji.com 能勢妙見山 妙見宗総本山 本瀧寺]
{{新宗教}}
{{Buddhism-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みょうけんしゅう}} [[Category:仏教系新宗教]] [[Category:日本の新宗教]] [[Category:日本の宗教団体]]

{{複数の問題 | 出典の明記 = 2020年1月 | 独自研究 = 2020年1月 }} '''妙見宗'''（みょうけんしゅう）とは、1946年12月10日に[[天台宗]]より独立した宗派。開祖は野間日照上人、宗祖は野間秀泉上人。総本山は[[本瀧寺]]。本瀧寺内にある滝で修行した[[修験者]]を中心に構成されている。本尊は本瀧寺は[[釈迦牟尼仏]]、末寺は[[法華曼荼羅]]。脇本尊として[[妙見菩薩|妙見大菩薩]]（みょうけんだいぼさつ）と[[常富大菩薩]]（つねとみだいぼさつ）。経典は[[妙法蓮華経]]。 総本山本瀧寺では[[日蓮宗]]の法要形式に[[台密]]と[[修験道]]を組み合わせた法要を行う。 妙見宗僧侶の服装は日蓮宗を模し[[お題目]]を唱えてはいるが、 本山である本瀧寺には[[法華曼荼羅]]が存在しないこと、[[日蓮教学]]が存在しないこと、[[日蓮]]聖人に関する記念行事が無いこと、荒行が無く日蓮宗と同等の法華祈祷の相承が無いこと、独自に日蓮宗の木剱相承を簡略化した祈祷を行うことなどから、日蓮宗の法要形式を修験道に取り入れた新宗教であるといえる。 一部末寺のみ日蓮宗の荒行成満者が存在する。

{{Multiple issues | References = January 2020 | Original research = January 2020 }} '''Myoken-shu''' (妙見宗) is a Buddhist sect that became independent from the [[Tendai sect]] on December 10, 1946. It is a member of the [[All Japan Buddhist Federation]] (全日本仏教会).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu {{!}} All Japan Buddhist Federation |url=http://www.jbf.ne.jp/member?id=15472 |website=www.jbf.ne.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14}}</ref> The sect was founded by Shonin Noma Nissho,<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu Headquarters Hon-Taki-ji｜Religious Organization Database |url=http://www.rirc.or.jp/xoops/modules/xxxxx05/detail.php?id=258 |website=www.rirc.or.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14 |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions}}</ref> with Shonin Noma Shusen as its patriarch. Its headquarters is located at [[Hon-Taki-ji]] Temple. The sect primarily consists of [[Shugenja]] (ascetics) who practiced at the waterfall within the temple grounds. The principal deity is [[Shakyamuni Buddha]],<ref name=":0" /> with secondary deities [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) and [[Jotomi Daibosatsu]].<ref name=":0" /> The sect's main scripture is the [[Lotus Sutra]] (妙法蓮華経).<ref name=":0" /> At the headquarters Hon-Taki-ji, rituals combine the liturgical style of the [[Nichiren sect]] with elements of [[Esoteric Tendai Buddhism]] (台密) and [[Shugendo]].
Shonin Noma Nissho received permission in June 1921 to relocate the temple named Kunko-in, which was renamed "Hon-Taki-ji" in November 1926.<ref name=":0" /> The main hall was completed in 1935.<ref name=":0" /> On December 10, 1945, the sect formally transitioned from the Tendai sect to establish the new sect "Myoken-shu," which continues to the present day.<ref name=":0" />
While the attire of Myoken-shu clergy resembles that of Nichiren priests, and practitioners chant the [[Odaimoku]] (sacred chant of Nichiren Buddhism), the sect exhibits significant differences. The Hon-Taki-ji headquarters lacks the [[Mandala of the Lotus Sutra]] (法華曼荼羅), there is no formal [[Nichiren doctrine]], no commemorative events for Saint [[Nichiren]], and no rigorous ascetic training (荒行). Furthermore, the sect does not inherit the traditional Nichiren esoteric prayer rituals (法華祈祷) but instead conducts simplified rituals based on its own adaptations of Nichiren practices. Some affiliate temples have priests who have completed Nichiren-style ascetic training, but this is rare.
== Notes == {{reflist}}

== External Links == * [http://www.hontakiji.com Nose Myokenzan Myoken-shu Headquarters Hon-Taki-ji]

Shonin Noma Nissho received permission in June 1921 to relocate the temple named Kunko-in, which was renamed "Hon-Taki-ji" in November 1926.<ref name=":0" /> The main hall was completed in 1935.<ref name=":0" /> On December 10, 1945, the sect formally transitioned from the Tendai sect to establish the new sect "Myoken-shu," which continues to the present day.<ref name=":0" />
While the attire of Myoken-shu clergy resembles that of Nichiren priests, and practitioners chant the [[Odaimoku]] (sacred chant of Nichiren Buddhism), the sect exhibits significant differences. The Hon-Taki-ji headquarters lacks the [[Mandala of the Lotus Sutra]] (法華曼荼羅), there is no formal [[Nichiren doctrine]], no commemorative events for Saint [[Nichiren]], and no rigorous ascetic training (荒行). Furthermore, the sect does not inherit the traditional Nichiren esoteric prayer rituals (法華祈祷) but instead conducts simplified rituals based on its own adaptations of Nichiren practices. Some affiliate temples have priests who have completed Nichiren-style ascetic training, but this is rare.

The principal deity is [[Shakyamuni Buddha]],<ref name=":0" /> with secondary deities [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) and [[Jotomi Daibosatsu]].<ref name=":0" /> The sect's main scripture is the [[Lotus Sutra]] (妙法蓮華経).<ref name=":0" /> At the headquarters Hon-Taki-ji, rituals combine the liturgical style of the [[Nichiren sect]] with elements of [[Esoteric Tendai Buddhism]] (台密) and [[Shugendo]].
Shonin Noma Nissho received permission in June 1921 to relocate the temple named Kunko-in, which was renamed "Hon-Taki-ji" in November 1926.<ref name=":0" /> The main hall was completed in 1935.<ref name=":0" /> On December 10, 1945, the sect formally transitioned from the Tendai sect to establish the new sect "Myoken-shu," which continues to the present day.<ref name=":0" />
While the attire of Myoken-shu clergy resembles that of Nichiren priests, and practitioners chant the [[Odaimoku]] (sacred chant of Nichiren Buddhism), the sect exhibits significant differences. The Hon-Taki-ji headquarters lacks the [[Mandala of the Lotus Sutra]] (法華曼荼羅), there is no formal [[Nichiren doctrine]], no commemorative events for Saint [[Nichiren]], and no rigorous ascetic training (荒行). Furthermore, the sect does not inherit the traditional Nichiren esoteric prayer rituals (法華祈祷) but instead conducts simplified rituals based on its own adaptations of Nichiren practices. Some affiliate temples have priests who have completed Nichiren-style ascetic training, but this is rare.

{{Multiple issues | References = January 2020 | Original research = January 2020 }} '''Myoken-shu''' (妙見宗) is a Buddhist sect that became independent from the [[Tendai sect]] on December 10, 1946. It is a member of the [[All Japan Buddhist Federation]] (全日本仏教会).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu {{!}} All Japan Buddhist Federation |url=http://www.jbf.ne.jp/member?id=15472 |website=www.jbf.ne.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14}}</ref> The sect was founded by Shonin Noma Nissho,<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu Headquarters Hon-Taki-ji｜Religious Organization Database |url=http://www.rirc.or.jp/xoops/modules/xxxxx05/detail.php?id=258 |website=www.rirc.or.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14 |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions}}</ref> with Shonin Noma Shusen as its patriarch. Its headquarters is located at [[Hon-Taki-ji]] Temple. The sect primarily consists of [[Shugenja]] (ascetics) who practiced at the waterfall within the temple grounds. The principal deity is [[Shakyamuni Buddha]],<ref name=":0" /> with secondary deities [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) and [[Jotomi Daibosatsu]].<ref name=":0" /> The sect's main scripture is the [[Lotus Sutra]] (妙法蓮華経).<ref name=":0" /> At the headquarters Hon-Taki-ji, rituals combine the liturgical style of the [[Nichiren sect]] with elements of [[Esoteric Tendai Buddhism]] (台密) and [[Shugendo]].
Shonin Noma Nissho received permission in June 1921 to relocate the temple named Kunko-in, which was renamed "Hon-Taki-ji" in November 1926.<ref name=":0" /> The main hall was completed in 1935.<ref name=":0" /> On December 10, 1945, the sect formally transitioned from the Tendai sect to establish the new sect "Myoken-shu," which continues to the present day.<ref name=":0" />
While the attire of Myoken-shu clergy resembles that of Nichiren priests, and practitioners chant the [[Odaimoku]] (sacred chant of Nichiren Buddhism), the sect exhibits significant differences. The Hon-Taki-ji headquarters lacks the [[Mandala of the Lotus Sutra]] (法華曼荼羅), there is no formal [[Nichiren doctrine]], no commemorative events for Saint [[Nichiren]], and no rigorous ascetic training (荒行). Furthermore, the sect does not inherit the traditional Nichiren esoteric prayer rituals (法華祈祷) but instead conducts simplified rituals based on its own adaptations of Nichiren practices. Some affiliate temples have priests who have completed Nichiren-style ascetic training, but this is rare.
== Notes == {{reflist}}

== External Links == * [http://www.hontakiji.com Nose Myokenzan Myoken-shu Headquarters Hon-Taki-ji]

{{Multiple issues | References = January 2020 | Original research = January 2020 }} '''Myoken-shu''' (妙見宗) is a Buddhist sect that became independent from the [[Tendai sect]] on December 10, 1946. It is a member of the [[All Japan Buddhist Federation]] (全日本仏教会).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu {{!}} All Japan Buddhist Federation |url=http://www.jbf.ne.jp/member?id=15472 |website=www.jbf.ne.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14}}</ref> The sect was founded by Shonin Noma Nissho,<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu Headquarters Hon-Taki-ji｜Religious Organization Database |url=http://www.rirc.or.jp/xoops/modules/xxxxx05/detail.php?id=258 |website=www.rirc.or.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14 |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions}}</ref> with Shonin Noma Shusen as its patriarch. Its headquarters is located at [[Hon-Taki-ji]] Temple. The sect primarily consists of [[Shugenja]] (ascetics) who practiced at the waterfall within the temple grounds. The principal deity is [[Shakyamuni Buddha]],<ref name=":0" /> with secondary deities [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) and [[Jotomi Daibosatsu]].<ref name=":0" /> The sect's main scripture is the [[Lotus Sutra]] (妙法蓮華経).<ref name=":0" /> At the headquarters Hon-Taki-ji, rituals combine the liturgical style of the [[Nichiren sect]] with elements of [[Esoteric Tendai Buddhism]] (台密) and [[Shugendo]].
Shonin Noma Nissho received permission in June 1921 to relocate the temple named Kunko-in, which was renamed "Hon-Taki-ji" in November 1926.<ref name=":0" /> The main hall was completed in 1935.<ref name=":0" /> On December 10, 1945, the sect formally transitioned from the Tendai sect to establish the new sect "Myoken-shu," which continues to the present day.<ref name=":0" />
While the attire of Myoken-shu clergy resembles that of Nichiren priests, and practitioners chant the [[Odaimoku]] (sacred chant of Nichiren Buddhism), the sect exhibits significant differences. The Hon-Taki-ji headquarters lacks the [[Mandala of the Lotus Sutra]] (法華曼荼羅), there is no formal [[Nichiren doctrine]], no commemorative events for Saint [[Nichiren]], and no rigorous ascetic training (荒行). Furthermore, the sect does not inherit the traditional Nichiren esoteric prayer rituals (法華祈祷) but instead conducts simplified rituals based on its own adaptations of Nichiren practices. Some affiliate temples have priests who have completed Nichiren-style ascetic training, but this is rare.

The principal deity is [[Shakyamuni Buddha]],<ref name=":0" /> with secondary deities [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) and [[Jotomi Daibosatsu]].<ref name=":0" /> The sect's main scripture is the [[Lotus Sutra]] (妙法蓮華経).<ref name=":0" /> At the headquarters Hon-Taki-ji, rituals combine the liturgical style of the [[Nichiren sect]] with elements of [[Esoteric Tendai Buddhism]] (台密) and [[Shugendo]].
Shonin Noma Nissho received permission in June 1921 to relocate the temple named Kunko-in, which was renamed "Hon-Taki-ji" in November 1926.<ref name=":0" /> The main hall was completed in 1935.<ref name=":0" /> On December 10, 1945, the sect formally transitioned from the Tendai sect to establish the new sect "Myoken-shu," which continues to the present day.<ref name=":0" />
While the attire of Myoken-shu clergy resembles that of Nichiren priests, and practitioners chant the [[Odaimoku]] (sacred chant of Nichiren Buddhism), the sect exhibits significant differences. The Hon-Taki-ji headquarters lacks the [[Mandala of the Lotus Sutra]] (法華曼荼羅), there is no formal [[Nichiren doctrine]], no commemorative events for Saint [[Nichiren]], and no rigorous ascetic training (荒行). Furthermore, the sect does not inherit the traditional Nichiren esoteric prayer rituals (法華祈祷) but instead conducts simplified rituals based on its own adaptations of Nichiren practices. Some affiliate temples have priests who have completed Nichiren-style ascetic training, but this is rare.

It is a member of the [[All Japan Buddhist Federation]] (全日本仏教会).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu {{!}} All Japan Buddhist Federation |url=http://www.jbf.ne.jp/member?id=15472 |website=www.jbf.ne.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14}}</ref> The sect was founded by Shonin Noma Nissho,<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu Headquarters Hon-Taki-ji｜Religious Organization Database |url=http://www.rirc.or.jp/xoops/modules/xxxxx05/detail.php?id=258 |website=www.rirc.or.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14 |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions}}</ref> with Shonin Noma Shusen as its patriarch. Its headquarters is located at [[Hon-Taki-ji]] Temple. The sect primarily consists of [[Shugenja]] (ascetics) who practiced at the waterfall within the temple grounds. The principal deity is [[Shakyamuni Buddha]],<ref name=":0" /> with secondary deities [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) and [[Jotomi Daibosatsu]].<ref name=":0" /> The sect's main scripture is the [[Lotus Sutra]] (妙法蓮華経).<ref name=":0" /> At the headquarters Hon-Taki-ji, rituals combine the liturgical style of the [[Nichiren sect]] with elements of [[Esoteric Tendai Buddhism]] (台密) and [[Shugendo]].
Shonin Noma Nissho received permission in June 1921 to relocate the temple named Kunko-in, which was renamed "Hon-Taki-ji" in November 1926.<ref name=":0" /> The main hall was completed in 1935.<ref name=":0" /> On December 10, 1945, the sect formally transitioned from the Tendai sect to establish the new sect "Myoken-shu," which continues to the present day.<ref name=":0" />
While the attire of Myoken-shu clergy resembles that of Nichiren priests, and practitioners chant the [[Odaimoku]] (sacred chant of Nichiren Buddhism), the sect exhibits significant differences. The Hon-Taki-ji headquarters lacks the [[Mandala of the Lotus Sutra]] (法華曼荼羅), there is no formal [[Nichiren doctrine]], no commemorative events for Saint [[Nichiren]], and no rigorous ascetic training (荒行). Furthermore, the sect does not inherit the traditional Nichiren esoteric prayer rituals (法華祈祷) but instead conducts simplified rituals based on its own adaptations of Nichiren practices. Some affiliate temples have priests who have completed Nichiren-style ascetic training, but this is rare.

{{Multiple issues | References = January 2020 | Original research = January 2020 }} '''Myoken-shu''' (妙見宗) is a Buddhist sect that became independent from the [[Tendai sect]] on December 10, 1946. It is a member of the [[All Japan Buddhist Federation]] (全日本仏教会).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu {{!}} All Japan Buddhist Federation |url=http://www.jbf.ne.jp/member?id=15472 |website=www.jbf.ne.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14}}</ref> The sect was founded by Shonin Noma Nissho,<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu Headquarters Hon-Taki-ji｜Religious Organization Database |url=http://www.rirc.or.jp/xoops/modules/xxxxx05/detail.php?id=258 |website=www.rirc.or.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14 |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions}}</ref> with Shonin Noma Shusen as its patriarch. Its headquarters is located at [[Hon-Taki-ji]] Temple. The sect primarily consists of [[Shugenja]] (ascetics) who practiced at the waterfall within the temple grounds. The principal deity is [[Shakyamuni Buddha]],<ref name=":0" /> with secondary deities [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) and [[Jotomi Daibosatsu]].<ref name=":0" /> The sect's main scripture is the [[Lotus Sutra]] (妙法蓮華経).<ref name=":0" /> At the headquarters Hon-Taki-ji, rituals combine the liturgical style of the [[Nichiren sect]] with elements of [[Esoteric Tendai Buddhism]] (台密) and [[Shugendo]].
Shonin Noma Nissho received permission in June 1921 to relocate the temple named Kunko-in, which was renamed "Hon-Taki-ji" in November 1926.<ref name=":0" /> The main hall was completed in 1935.<ref name=":0" /> On December 10, 1945, the sect formally transitioned from the Tendai sect to establish the new sect "Myoken-shu," which continues to the present day.<ref name=":0" />
While the attire of Myoken-shu clergy resembles that of Nichiren priests, and practitioners chant the [[Odaimoku]] (sacred chant of Nichiren Buddhism), the sect exhibits significant differences. The Hon-Taki-ji headquarters lacks the [[Mandala of the Lotus Sutra]] (法華曼荼羅), there is no formal [[Nichiren doctrine]], no commemorative events for Saint [[Nichiren]], and no rigorous ascetic training (荒行). Furthermore, the sect does not inherit the traditional Nichiren esoteric prayer rituals (法華祈祷) but instead conducts simplified rituals based on its own adaptations of Nichiren practices. Some affiliate temples have priests who have completed Nichiren-style ascetic training, but this is rare.

revision.text:
{{Multiple issues | References = January 2020 | Original research = January 2020 }} '''Myoken-shu''' (妙見宗) is a Buddhist sect that became independent from the [[Tendai sect]] on December 10, 1946. It is a member of the [[All Japan Buddhist Federation]] (全日本仏教会).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu {{!}} All Japan Buddhist Federation |url=http://www.jbf.ne.jp/member?id=15472 |website=www.jbf.ne.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14}}</ref> The sect was founded by Shonin Noma Nissho,<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu Headquarters Hon-Taki-ji｜Religious Organization Database |url=http://www.rirc.or.jp/xoops/modules/xxxxx05/detail.php?id=258 |website=www.rirc.or.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14 |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions}}</ref> with Shonin Noma Shusen as its patriarch. Its headquarters is located at [[Hon-Taki-ji]] Temple. The sect primarily consists of [[Shugenja]] (ascetics) who practiced at the waterfall within the temple grounds. The principal deity is [[Shakyamuni Buddha]],<ref name=":0" /> with secondary deities [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) and [[Jotomi Daibosatsu]].<ref name=":0" /> The sect's main scripture is the [[Lotus Sutra]] (妙法蓮華経).<ref name=":0" /> At the headquarters Hon-Taki-ji, rituals combine the liturgical style of the [[Nichiren sect]] with elements of [[Esoteric Tendai Buddhism]] (台密) and [[Shugendo]].
Shonin Noma Nissho received permission in June 1921 to relocate the temple named Kunko-in, which was renamed "Hon-Taki-ji" in November 1926.<ref name=":0" /> The main hall was completed in 1935.<ref name=":0" /> On December 10, 1945, the sect formally transitioned from the Tendai sect to establish the new sect "Myoken-shu," which continues to the present day.<ref name=":0" />
While the attire of Myoken-shu clergy resembles that of Nichiren priests, and practitioners chant the [[Odaimoku]] (sacred chant of Nichiren Buddhism), the sect exhibits significant differences. The Hon-Taki-ji headquarters lacks the [[Mandala of the Lotus Sutra]] (法華曼荼羅), there is no formal [[Nichiren doctrine]], no commemorative events for Saint [[Nichiren]], and no rigorous ascetic training (荒行). Furthermore, the sect does not inherit the traditional Nichiren esoteric prayer rituals (法華祈祷) but instead conducts simplified rituals based on its own adaptations of Nichiren practices. Some affiliate temples have priests who have completed Nichiren-style ascetic training, but this is rare.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * [[Hon-Taki-ji]] * [[Nose Myokenzan]]
== External Links == * [http://www.hontakiji.com Nose Myokenzan Myoken-shu Headquarters Hon-Taki-ji]

{{Multiple issues | References = January 2020 | Original research = January 2020 }} '''Myoken-shu''' (妙見宗) is a Buddhist sect that became independent from the [[Tendai sect]] on December 10, 1946. It is a member of the [[All Japan Buddhist Federation]] (全日本仏教会).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu {{!}} All Japan Buddhist Federation |url=http://www.jbf.ne.jp/member?id=15472 |website=www.jbf.ne.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14}}</ref> The sect was founded by Shonin Noma Nissho,<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu Headquarters Hon-Taki-ji｜Religious Organization Database |url=http://www.rirc.or.jp/xoops/modules/xxxxx05/detail.php?id=258 |website=www.rirc.or.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14 |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions}}</ref> with Shonin Noma Shusen as its patriarch. Its headquarters is located at [[Hon-Taki-ji]] Temple. The sect primarily consists of [[Shugenja]] (ascetics) who practiced at the waterfall within the temple grounds. The principal deity is [[Shakyamuni Buddha]],<ref name=":0" /> with secondary deities [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) and [[Jotomi Daibosatsu]].<ref name=":0" /> The sect's main scripture is the [[Lotus Sutra]] (妙法蓮華経).<ref name=":0" /> At the headquarters Hon-Taki-ji, rituals combine the liturgical style of the [[Nichiren sect]] with elements of [[Esoteric Tendai Buddhism]] (台密) and [[Shugendo]].
Shonin Noma Nissho received permission in June 1921 to relocate the temple named Kunko-in, which was renamed "Hon-Taki-ji" in November 1926.<ref name=":0" /> The main hall was completed in 1935.<ref name=":0" /> On December 10, 1945, the sect formally transitioned from the Tendai sect to establish the new sect "Myoken-shu," which continues to the present day.<ref name=":0" />
While the attire of Myoken-shu clergy resembles that of Nichiren priests, and practitioners chant the [[Odaimoku]] (sacred chant of Nichiren Buddhism), the sect exhibits significant differences. The Hon-Taki-ji headquarters lacks the [[Mandala of the Lotus Sutra]] (法華曼荼羅), there is no formal [[Nichiren doctrine]], no commemorative events for Saint [[Nichiren]], and no rigorous ascetic training (荒行). Furthermore, the sect does not inherit the traditional Nichiren esoteric prayer rituals (法華祈祷) but instead conducts simplified rituals based on its own adaptations of Nichiren practices. Some affiliate temples have priests who have completed Nichiren-style ascetic training, but this is rare.

Furthermore, the sect does not inherit the traditional Nichiren esoteric prayer rituals (法華祈祷) but instead conducts simplified rituals based on its own adaptations of Nichiren practices. Some affiliate temples have priests who have completed Nichiren-style ascetic training, but this is rare.

{{Multiple issues | References = January 2020 | Original research = January 2020 }} '''Myoken-shu''' (妙見宗) is a Buddhist sect that became independent from the [[Tendai sect]] on December 10, 1946. It is a member of the [[All Japan Buddhist Federation]] (全日本仏教会).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu {{!}} All Japan Buddhist Federation |url=http://www.jbf.ne.jp/member?id=15472 |website=www.jbf.ne.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14}}</ref> The sect was founded by Shonin Noma Nissho,<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu Headquarters Hon-Taki-ji｜Religious Organization Database |url=http://www.rirc.or.jp/xoops/modules/xxxxx05/detail.php?id=258 |website=www.rirc.or.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14 |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions}}</ref> with Shonin Noma Shusen as its patriarch. Its headquarters is located at [[Hon-Taki-ji]] Temple. The sect primarily consists of [[Shugenja]] (ascetics) who practiced at the waterfall within the temple grounds. The principal deity is [[Shakyamuni Buddha]],<ref name=":0" /> with secondary deities [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) and [[Jotomi Daibosatsu]].<ref name=":0" /> The sect's main scripture is the [[Lotus Sutra]] (妙法蓮華経).<ref name=":0" /> At the headquarters Hon-Taki-ji, rituals combine the liturgical style of the [[Nichiren sect]] with elements of [[Esoteric Tendai Buddhism]] (台密) and [[Shugendo]].
Shonin Noma Nissho received permission in June 1921 to relocate the temple named Kunko-in, which was renamed "Hon-Taki-ji" in November 1926.<ref name=":0" /> The main hall was completed in 1935.<ref name=":0" /> On December 10, 1945, the sect formally transitioned from the Tendai sect to establish the new sect "Myoken-shu," which continues to the present day.<ref name=":0" />
While the attire of Myoken-shu clergy resembles that of Nichiren priests, and practitioners chant the [[Odaimoku]] (sacred chant of Nichiren Buddhism), the sect exhibits significant differences. The Hon-Taki-ji headquarters lacks the [[Mandala of the Lotus Sutra]] (法華曼荼羅), there is no formal [[Nichiren doctrine]], no commemorative events for Saint [[Nichiren]], and no rigorous ascetic training (荒行). Furthermore, the sect does not inherit the traditional Nichiren esoteric prayer rituals (法華祈祷) but instead conducts simplified rituals based on its own adaptations of Nichiren practices. Some affiliate temples have priests who have completed Nichiren-style ascetic training, but this is rare.

Its headquarters is located at [[Hon-Taki-ji]] Temple. The sect primarily consists of [[Shugenja]] (ascetics) who practiced at the waterfall within the temple grounds. The principal deity is [[Shakyamuni Buddha]],<ref name=":0" /> with secondary deities [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) and [[Jotomi Daibosatsu]].<ref name=":0" /> The sect's main scripture is the [[Lotus Sutra]] (妙法蓮華経).<ref name=":0" /> At the headquarters Hon-Taki-ji, rituals combine the liturgical style of the [[Nichiren sect]] with elements of [[Esoteric Tendai Buddhism]] (台密) and [[Shugendo]].
Shonin Noma Nissho received permission in June 1921 to relocate the temple named Kunko-in, which was renamed "Hon-Taki-ji" in November 1926.<ref name=":0" /> The main hall was completed in 1935.<ref name=":0" /> On December 10, 1945, the sect formally transitioned from the Tendai sect to establish the new sect "Myoken-shu," which continues to the present day.<ref name=":0" />

The principal deity is [[Shakyamuni Buddha]],<ref name=":0" /> with secondary deities [[Myoken Bosatsu]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) and [[Jotomi Daibosatsu]].<ref name=":0" /> The sect's main scripture is the [[Lotus Sutra]] (妙法蓮華経).<ref name=":0" /> At the headquarters Hon-Taki-ji, rituals combine the liturgical style of the [[Nichiren sect]] with elements of [[Esoteric Tendai Buddhism]] (台密) and [[Shugendo]].
Shonin Noma Nissho received permission in June 1921 to relocate the temple named Kunko-in, which was renamed "Hon-Taki-ji" in November 1926.<ref name=":0" /> The main hall was completed in 1935.<ref name=":0" /> On December 10, 1945, the sect formally transitioned from the Tendai sect to establish the new sect "Myoken-shu," which continues to the present day.<ref name=":0" />
While the attire of Myoken-shu clergy resembles that of Nichiren priests, and practitioners chant the [[Odaimoku]] (sacred chant of Nichiren Buddhism), the sect exhibits significant differences. The Hon-Taki-ji headquarters lacks the [[Mandala of the Lotus Sutra]] (法華曼荼羅), there is no formal [[Nichiren doctrine]], no commemorative events for Saint [[Nichiren]], and no rigorous ascetic training (荒行). Furthermore, the sect does not inherit the traditional Nichiren esoteric prayer rituals (法華祈祷) but instead conducts simplified rituals based on its own adaptations of Nichiren practices. Some affiliate temples have priests who have completed Nichiren-style ascetic training, but this is rare.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:妙見宗]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11447017]]
Wikidata name: Myōken-shū
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Multiple issues
| References = January 2020
| Original research = January 2020
}}
'''Myoken-shu''' (妙見宗) is a Buddhist sect that became independent from the [[Tendai sect]] on December 10, 1946. It is a member of the [[All Japan Buddhist Federation]] (全日本仏教会).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu {{!}} All Japan Buddhist Federation |url=http://www.jbf.ne.jp/member?id=15472 |website=www.jbf.ne.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14}}</ref> The sect was founded by Shonin Noma Nissho,<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu Headquarters Hon-Taki-ji｜Religious Organization Database |url=http://www.rirc.or.jp/xoops/modules/xxxxx05/detail.php?id=258 |website=www.rirc.or.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14 |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions}}</ref> with Shonin Noma Shusen as its patriarch. Its headquarters is located at [[Hon-Taki-ji]] Temple. The sect primarily consists of [[:en:Shugenja|Shugenja]] (ascetics) who practiced at the waterfall within the temple grounds. The principal deity is [[:en:Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha]],<ref name=":0" /> with secondary deities [[:en:Myōken|Myōken]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) and [[Jotomi Daibosatsu]].<ref name=":0" /> The sect's main scripture is the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]] (妙法蓮華経).<ref name=":0" /> At the headquarters Hon-Taki-ji, rituals combine the liturgical style of the [[:en:Nichiren sect|Nichiren sect]] with elements of [[Esoteric Tendai Buddhism]] (台密) and [[Shugendō|Shugendo]].

Shonin Noma Nissho received permission in June 1921 to relocate the temple named Kunko-in, which was renamed "Hon-Taki-ji" in November 1926.<ref name=":0" /> The main hall was completed in 1935.<ref name=":0" /> On December 10, 1945, the sect formally transitioned from the Tendai sect to establish the new sect "Myoken-shu," which continues to the present day.<ref name=":0" />

While the attire of Myoken-shu clergy resembles that of Nichiren priests, and practitioners chant the [[:en:Odaimoku|Odaimoku]] (sacred chant of Nichiren Buddhism), the sect exhibits significant differences. The Hon-Taki-ji headquarters lacks the [[Mandala of the Lotus Sutra]] (法華曼荼羅), there is no formal [[Nichiren doctrine]], no commemorative events for Saint [[:en:Nichiren|Nichiren]], and no rigorous ascetic training (荒行). Furthermore, the sect does not inherit the traditional Nichiren esoteric prayer rituals (法華祈祷) but instead conducts simplified rituals based on its own adaptations of Nichiren practices. Some affiliate temples have priests who have completed Nichiren-style ascetic training, but this is rare.

== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== See Also ==
* [[Hon-Taki-ji]]
* [[Nose Myokenzan]]

== External Links ==
* [http://www.hontakiji.com Nose Myokenzan Myoken-shu Headquarters Hon-Taki-ji]

{{Buddhism-stub}}
{{New religious movement}}

==References==
{{reflist}}
{{Myōken Faith}}

==References==
{{reflist}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11447017]]
Wikidata name: Myōken-shū
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Multiple issues
| References = January 2020
| Original research = January 2020
}}
'''Myoken-shu''' (妙見宗) is a Buddhist sect that became independent from the [[Tendai sect]] on December 10, 1946. It is a member of the [[All Japan Buddhist Federation]] (全日本仏教会).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu {{!}} All Japan Buddhist Federation |url=http://www.jbf.ne.jp/member?id=15472 |website=www.jbf.ne.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14}}</ref> The sect was founded by Shonin Noma Nissho,<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu Headquarters Hon-Taki-ji｜Religious Organization Database |url=http://www.rirc.or.jp/xoops/modules/xxxxx05/detail.php?id=258 |website=www.rirc.or.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14 |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions}}</ref> with Shonin Noma Shusen as its patriarch. Its headquarters is located at [[Hon-Taki-ji]] Temple. The sect primarily consists of [[:en:Shugenja|Shugenja]] (ascetics) who practiced at the waterfall within the temple grounds. The principal deity is [[:en:Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha]],<ref name=":0" /> with secondary deities [[:en:Myōken|Myōken]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) and [[Jotomi Daibosatsu]].<ref name=":0" /> The sect's main scripture is the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]] (妙法蓮華経).<ref name=":0" /> At the headquarters Hon-Taki-ji, rituals combine the liturgical style of the [[:en:Nichiren sect|Nichiren sect]] with elements of [[Esoteric Tendai Buddhism]] (台密) and [[Shugendō|Shugendo]].

Shonin Noma Nissho received permission in June 1921 to relocate the temple named Kunko-in, which was renamed "Hon-Taki-ji" in November 1926.<ref name=":0" /> The main hall was completed in 1935.<ref name=":0" /> On December 10, 1945, the sect formally transitioned from the Tendai sect to establish the new sect "Myoken-shu," which continues to the present day.<ref name=":0" />

While the attire of Myoken-shu clergy resembles that of Nichiren priests, and practitioners chant the [[:en:Odaimoku|Odaimoku]] (sacred chant of Nichiren Buddhism), the sect exhibits significant differences. The Hon-Taki-ji headquarters lacks the [[Mandala of the Lotus Sutra]] (法華曼荼羅), there is no formal [[Nichiren doctrine]], no commemorative events for Saint [[:en:Nichiren|Nichiren]], and no rigorous ascetic training (荒行). Furthermore, the sect does not inherit the traditional Nichiren esoteric prayer rituals (法華祈祷) but instead conducts simplified rituals based on its own adaptations of Nichiren practices. Some affiliate temples have priests who have completed Nichiren-style ascetic training, but this is rare.

== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== See Also ==
* [[Hon-Taki-ji]]
* [[Nose Myokenzan]]

== External Links ==
* [http://www.hontakiji.com Nose Myokenzan Myoken-shu Headquarters Hon-Taki-ji]

{{Buddhism-stub}}
{{New religious movement}}

'''Myoken-shu''' (妙見宗) is a Buddhist sect that became independent from the [[Tendai sect]] on December 10, 1946. It is a member of the [[All Japan Buddhist Federation]] (全日本仏教会).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu {{!}} All Japan Buddhist Federation |url=http://www.jbf.ne.jp/member?id=15472 |website=www.jbf.ne.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14}}</ref> The sect was founded by Shonin Noma Nissho,<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu Headquarters Hon-Taki-ji｜Religious Organization Database |url=http://www.rirc.or.jp/xoops/modules/xxxxx05/detail.php?id=258 |website=www.rirc.or.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14 |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions}}</ref> with Shonin Noma Shusen as its patriarch. Its headquarters is located at [[Hon-Taki-ji]] Temple. The sect primarily consists of [[:en:Shugenja|Shugenja]] (ascetics) who practiced at the waterfall within the temple grounds. The principal deity is [[:en:Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha]],<ref name=":0" /> with secondary deities [[:en:Myōken|Myōken]] (Myoken Daibosatsu) and [[Jotomi Daibosatsu]].<ref name=":0" /> The sect's main scripture is the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]] (妙法蓮華経).<ref name=":0" /> At the headquarters Hon-Taki-ji, rituals combine the liturgical style of the [[:en:Nichiren sect|Nichiren sect]] with elements of [[Esoteric Tendai Buddhism]] (台密) and [[Shugendō|Shugendo]].

Shonin Noma Nissho received permission in June 1921 to relocate the temple named Kunko-in, which was renamed "Hon-Taki-ji" in November 1926.<ref name=":0" /> The main hall was completed in 1935.<ref name=":0" /> On December 10, 1945, the sect formally transitioned from the Tendai sect to establish the new sect "Myoken-shu," which continues to the present day.<ref name=":0" />

While the attire of Myoken-shu clergy resembles that of Nichiren priests, and practitioners chant the [[:en:Odaimoku|Odaimoku]] (sacred chant of Nichiren Buddhism), the sect exhibits significant differences. The Hon-Taki-ji headquarters lacks the [[Mandala of the Lotus Sutra]] (法華曼荼羅), there is no formal [[Nichiren doctrine]], no commemorative events for Saint [[:en:Nichiren|Nichiren]], and no rigorous ascetic training (荒行). Furthermore, the sect does not inherit the traditional Nichiren esoteric prayer rituals (法華祈祷) but instead conducts simplified rituals based on its own adaptations of Nichiren practices. Some affiliate temples have priests who have completed Nichiren-style ascetic training, but this is rare.

The Hon-Taki-ji headquarters lacks the [[Mandala of the Lotus Sutra]] (法華曼荼羅), there is no formal [[Nichiren doctrine]], no commemorative events for Saint {{ill|Nichiren|1=Nichiren|2=ja|3=日蓮|4=ko|5=니치렌|6=de|7=Nichiren|8=es|9=Nichiren|10=fr|11=Nichiren|12=it|13=Nichiren|14=nl|15=Nichiren|16=no|17=Nichiren|lt=Nichiren|WD=Q311478en|Nichiren|1=Nichiren|2=ja|3=日蓮|4=ko|5=니치렌|6=de|7=Nichiren|8=es|9=Nichiren|10=fr|11=Nichiren|12=it|13=Nichiren|14=nl|15=Nichiren|16=no|17=Nichiren|lt=Nichiren|WD=Q311478WD=Q311478|lt=Nichiren}}, and no rigorous ascetic training (荒行). Furthermore, the sect does not inherit the traditional Nichiren esoteric prayer rituals (法華祈祷) but instead conducts simplified rituals based on its own adaptations of Nichiren practices. Some affiliate temples have priests who have completed Nichiren-style ascetic training, but this is rare.

Its headquarters is located at [[Hon-Taki-ji]] Temple. The sect primarily consists of {{ill|Shugenja|en|Shugenja|WD=Q7504680|lt=Shugenja}} (ascetics) who practiced at the waterfall within the temple grounds. The principal deity is [[:en:Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha]],<ref name=":0" /> with secondary deities {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|WD=Q10960623|lt=Myōken}} (Myoken Daibosatsu) and [[Jotomi Daibosatsu]].<ref name=":0" /> The sect's main scripture is the {{ill|en:Lotus Sutra|en|en:Lotus Sutra|WD=Q861212|lt=en:Lotus Sutra}} (妙法蓮華経).<ref name=":0" /> At the headquarters Hon-Taki-ji, rituals combine the liturgical style of the [[:en:Nichiren sect|Nichiren sect]] with elements of [[Esoteric Tendai Buddhism]] (台密) and [[Shugendō|Shugendo]].

The Hon-Taki-ji headquarters lacks the [[Mandala of the Lotus Sutra]] (法華曼荼羅), there is no formal [[Nichiren doctrine]], no commemorative events for Saint {{ill|Nichiren|1=Nichiren|2=ja|3=日蓮|4=ko|5=니치렌|6=de|7=Nichiren|8=es|9=Nichiren|10=fr|11=Nichiren|12=it|13=Nichiren|14=nl|15=Nichiren|16=no|17=Nichiren|lt=Nichiren|WD=Q311478en|Nichiren|1=Nichiren|2=ja|3=日蓮|4=ko|5=니치렌|6=de|7=Nichiren|8=es|9=Nichiren|10=fr|11=Nichiren|12=it|13=Nichiren|14=nl|15=Nichiren|16=no|17=Nichiren|lt=Nichiren|WD=Q311478WD=Q311478|lt=Nichiren}}, and no rigorous ascetic training (荒行). Furthermore, the sect does not inherit the traditional Nichiren esoteric prayer rituals (法華祈祷) but instead conducts simplified rituals based on its own adaptations of Nichiren practices. Some affiliate temples have priests who have completed Nichiren-style ascetic training, but this is rare.

{{Multiple issues | References = January 2020 | Original research = January 2020 }} '''Myoken-shu''' (妙見宗) is a Buddhist sect that became independent from the [[Tendai sect]] on December 10, 1946. It is a member of the [[All Japan Buddhist Federation]] (全日本仏教会).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu {{!}} All Japan Buddhist Federation |url=http://www.jbf.ne.jp/member?id=15472 |website=www.jbf.ne.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14}}</ref> The sect was founded by Shonin Noma Nissho,<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Myoken-shu Headquarters Hon-Taki-ji｜Religious Organization Database |url=http://www.rirc.or.jp/xoops/modules/xxxxx05/detail.php?id=258 |website=www.rirc.or.jp |accessdate=2022-02-14 |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions}}</ref> with Shonin Noma Shusen as its patriarch. Its headquarters is located at [[Hon-Taki-ji]] Temple. The sect primarily consists of {{ill|Shugenja|en|Shugenja|qid=Q7504680|lt=Shugenja|1=Shugenja}} (ascetics) who practiced at the waterfall within the temple grounds. The principal deity is [[:en:Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha]],<ref name=":0" /> with secondary deities {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|qid=Q10960623|lt=Myōken|1=Myōken}} (Myoken Daibosatsu) and [[Jotomi Daibosatsu]].<ref name=":0" /> The sect's main scripture is the {{ill|en:Lotus Sutra|en|en:Lotus Sutra|qid=Q861212|lt=en:Lotus Sutra|1=Lotus Sutra}} (妙法蓮華経).<ref name=":0" /> At the headquarters Hon-Taki-ji, rituals combine the liturgical style of the [[:en:Nichiren sect|Nichiren sect]] with elements of [[Esoteric Tendai Buddhism]] (台密) and [[Shugendō|Shugendo]].
Shonin Noma Nissho received permission in June 1921 to relocate the temple named Kunko-in, which was renamed "Hon-Taki-ji" in November 1926.<ref name=":0" /> The main hall was completed in 1935.<ref name=":0" /> On December 10, 1945, the sect formally transitioned from the Tendai sect to establish the new sect "Myoken-shu," which continues to the present day.<ref name=":0" />
While the attire of Myoken-shu clergy resembles that of Nichiren priests, and practitioners chant the [[:en:Odaimoku|Odaimoku]] (sacred chant of Nichiren Buddhism), the sect exhibits significant differences. The Hon-Taki-ji headquarters lacks the [[Mandala of the Lotus Sutra]] (法華曼荼羅), there is no formal [[Nichiren doctrine]], no commemorative events for Saint {{ill|Nichiren|1=Nichiren|2=ja|3=日蓮|4=ko|5=니치렌|6=de|7=Nichiren|8=es|9=Nichiren|10=fr|11=Nichiren|12=it|13=Nichiren|14=nl|15=Nichiren|16=no|17=Nichiren|lt=Nichiren|qid=Q311478en|Nichiren|1=Nichiren|2=ja|3=日蓮|4=ko|5=니치렌|6=de|7=Nichiren|8=es|9=Nichiren|10=fr|11=Nichiren|12=it|13=Nichiren|14=nl|15=Nichiren|16=no|17=Nichiren|lt=Nichiren|qid=Q311478qid=Q311478|lt=Nichiren}}, and no rigorous ascetic training (荒行). Furthermore, the sect does not inherit the traditional Nichiren esoteric prayer rituals (法華祈祷) but instead conducts simplified rituals based on its own adaptations of Nichiren practices. Some affiliate temples have priests who have completed Nichiren-style ascetic training, but this is rare.
